title: YIPING YIN Z5343015
body:
FINS3616 Individual Assignment – AES

                                                         Yiping Yin (z5343015)



                   1. Estimating the currency betas

                   Linear regression analysis is constructed using the equation (2) of below:

                                       𝑅!,# − 𝑅$,# = 𝛼 + 𝛽%&# ∗ (𝑅%&#,# − 𝑅$,# ) + 𝛽'( ∗ 𝑅'(,# + 𝜀!,#

                   New variables (arithmetic monthly return, market return, DXY return, AES excess

                   return, and market excess return) have been created to run regression analysis.

SUMMARY

OUTPUT



Regression Statistics


Multiple R                    0.504

R Square                      0.254


Adjusted R                    0.249

Square


Standard Error                0.117

Observations                   268



ANOVA

                         df                 SS             MS        F       Significance F


Regression                         2             1.230     0.615    45.164             0.000

Residual                       265               3.610     0.014


Total                          267               4.840



                    Coefficients       Standard Error     t Stat   P-value    Lower 95%        Upper 95%    Lower 95.0%       Upper 95.0%


Intercept                     0.000              0.007     0.004     0.997            -0.014        0.014         -0.014             0.014

Market                        1.285              0.176     7.314     0.000             0.939        1.631          0.939             1.631


FX                        -0.974                 0.345    -2.823     0.005            -1.654       -0.295         -1.654            -0.295




                                                                                                                          1
 Excess return being the dependent variable, and market excess return and FX return

being independent variables. Workings being constructed in the ‘Section 3.4’

worksheet, with regression output is shown above. Result depicts that the estimated

market beta equals to 1.285, the currency beta equals to -0.974, and adjusted R-

square being 0.249 (round to three decimal places). This indicates that AES is more

volatile (𝛽%&# =1.285>1) than the market and moves approximately 1.285 times than

the market. The -0.974 FX beta discloses that AES’s stock performance being

worsened by the appreciation of local currency. Namely, AES will be worse off when

the NXY appreciates and vice versa. The adjusted R-square implies that the

constructed regression model explains less than one-quarter of the variation in the

excess return.

2. Interpreting the results

For the AES stock:

•   The coefficient of the DXY return (𝛽'( ) being -0.974, indicating that additional

    percentage of FX (appreciation) will result in the negative performance on the

    excessive return of the stock. The p-value < 0.01, indicating that the coefficient

    of the DXY return being statistically significant at 1% level.

    One percentage of the USD appreciation (which is the USD beta) will result in

    the excess return of AES to reduce by 0.974. This complies with the stock

    performance under FX exposure, where AES’s stock performance benefits with

    the USD depreciation.

    P-value equals to 0.005 and t-statistics being -2.823 (as shown in Section 1

    above). Results explain that the relationship being statistically significant at 5%.

•   Coefficient can be used for stock performance forecasting, implying the

    relationship between the stock performance and USD currency change. As

    indicated in the results above, the appreciation of USD result in the monthly

    excess return of AES to reduce by 97.4%. Therefore, an inverse FX exposure,

    further validating the claimant under the FX exposure paragraph.




                                                                                           2
 •   Market beta (𝛽%&# ) equals to 1.285 and currency beta (𝛽'( ) equals to -0.974. To

    determine the economic significance between these two, movement in excess

    return will be conducted:

                                Market return       DXY

                 Std dev                    0.044                  0.022

    One standard deviation change in market return will result in 1.285*0.044 =

    0.056 or 5.6% positive movement in excess return of AES.

    One standard deviation change in DXY will result in -0.974*0.022 = -0.022 or

    2.2% negative movement in excess return of AES.

    Therefore, market beta is a more economically meaningful factor compared to

    currency beta given its greater economic impact.

•   Adjusted R-square for the regression model being 0.249, implying that market

    and FX exposure only explain 24.9% of the stock excess return of AES. In terms

    of the goodness-of-fit, the regression model has moderate level of explanatory

    power and around three-fourths of the stock excess return of AES being

    explained by other factors not captured by the chosen regression model.

3. Explaining the currency betas with fundamentals

Estimated currency beta equals to -0.974, indicating a negative FX exposure. This

relates to the business scope and operational activities due to:

•   AES has business operations within the global scope, whereby it operates

    among different countries, including Argentina, Brazil, Bulgaria, Chile, Panama,

    etc., of which foreign currency transactions are engaged. As stated in the 2024

    annual report under note 28, 62% of the business revenue being international

    revenue in developing countries (AES 2024, p. 193) of which being denominated

    in local currency. Therefore, business activities result in AES being highly

    volatile to the foreign exchange exposure, especially when it is against USD

    during transactions. Though being the MNC in US, AES’s revenue is exposed to

    the foreign exchange fluctuation in terms of the foreign exchange transactions,

    thereby confirming the negative currency beta. With the depreciation of USD in



                                                                                        3
     2024 (Morgan Stanley 2025), AES gains positive foreign exchange gain as

    disclosed in the results of operation (AES 2024, p. 82).

•   There is material revenue fluctuation that results from the noticeable value

    changes and AES observes evident foreign currency transaction gains (losses)

    movements. As disclosed in the annual report, AES experiences significant

    movement from $359 million loss in 2023 to $31 million gain by 2024 (AES 2024,

    p. 86). Foreign exchange gain of $31 million is denominated in foreign currency

    (i.e. Brazilian real) and loss being resulted from the Argentine peso depreciation,

    suggesting that heavy reliance on foreign currency results in AES’s negative FX

    exposure. As foreign currency exchange rate affects business capability in

    hedging foreign currency risk (AES 2024, n.5), changes in sales valuation

    contains fluctuated FX exposure that complies with currency beta. Therefore, the

    appreciation of USD will result in AES exposed to the greater extent of foreign

    exchange loss, validating the negative FX exposure.

•   AES maintains a beneficial supplier relationship by employing the AES

    Corporation Supplier Code of Conduct (AES n.d.). With significant export to

    other countries where transactions being settled in wholesale and retail markets

    are in local currencies, AES thereby possessing revenue and cost of sales

    subject to foreign exchange exposure. Thus, USD depreciation will result in the

    AES stock to increase and thus imposes positive influence on the stock

    performance of AES.

•   The negative FX exposure also associates with the currency exposure on the

    balance sheet side, where its long-term receivables being denominated in

    Argentine pesos (AES 2024, p. 7). AES also engages in hedging activities with

    derivative instruments, with foreign currencies denominated in Chilean peso,

    Columbian peso, and Mexican peso as disclosed in note 6 (AES 2024, p. 146).

    There is evidence that the currency exposures imposed on the assets side of

    AES, with timing principle enlarges the FX exposure (De Oliveira 2012).

4. Predicting stock price performance in 2024



                                                                                       4

                  Result below shows that the enriched CAPM draws the forecasted stock return being

                  19.75%, whereas the real return being -22.84%. This results in a 42.59% forecasted

                  error by predicting positive stock return for 2024, yet there occurs a negative return

                  for 2024.

                                         Predicted stock return                     19.75%


                                         Real stock return                          -22.84%


                  The overprediction of the stock performance indicates the inaccuracy of the enriched

                  CAPM model, suggesting the introduced model fails to capture the influential

                  independent variables that affects the stock excess return. Conclusion aligns with the

                  adjusted R-square of 0.249, where the inaccuracy of the CAPM model being

                  observed.

                  5. Analyze the exchange risk

                  High volatility has been observed within the sample period (i.e. August 2001 to

                  December 2024), as depicted in the following diagram. Inflation, interest rate, and



                                                                                                                                 5
 trade and credit balance are the three theoretical drivers resulting in the DXY

movements.

Inflation differentials represent one prominent driver for the DXY movement.

Amounting to the expected exchange rate growth, inflation rate affects the

purchasing power and being the variation between economies. Increasing inflation

rate would imply the weakened DXY by eroding the purchasing power and negatively

affect the stability of the currency (Checherita-Westphal et al. 2025). For the case of

DXY, reducing inflation rate during 2001-2008 (as shown in Statista (2025)) lays the

fluctuated foundation of DXY movement. The subsequent increase results in DXY

experiencing in dynamic movement.




Overall, the increasing inflation rate indirectly co-moves with DXY, by accompanying

with the DXY appreciation, validating the inflation differential theory.

Interest rate differentials represent the second DXY driver within the designated

period. By revealing the forecast on currency depreciation, the increasing interest

                                                                                          6
 rate will attain capital inflow and attains capital investments (Belke et al. 2017),

whereby it further increases DXY with arising USD demand. The noticeable interest

rate increases in 2004, 2016, and 2022 (World Bank 2025) results in further increase

of DXY, resulting in the positive USD appreciation.




Therefore, a positive co-movement between DXY and interest rate has been derived,

with increasing interest rate contributes to increasing DXY.

The third explanatory variable being the trade and credit balance. A solid trade

position would positively benefit DXY and vice versa (Trinh 2014). During the period

(2001-2024), US experiences global financial crisis and pandemic impact, where the

economic recession has been attained. However, US still makes a solid trade

position and USD being the reserve currency within the global scope. In this case,

the trade balance results in positive co-movement with DXY by attaining the

achievement of DXY support.

6. Hedging exchange rate risk

To hedge against the foreign currency exposure from July 1 to December 15, 2025,

and within the USD 1 million investment limit on AES, a hedging strategy of

constructing portfolio of US Dollar Index Futures (ICE 2025) could be used. This is

chosen by complying with the regression equation (2), where the stock price of AES

yielding inverse relationship with USD appreciation. It also possesses direct USD

exposure and with standard contract size. Its high liquidity also lays the foundation to

enable flexible dealing with the holding instruments within the designated investment

horizon.


                                                                                       7
 This future option is considered to mitigate the Dollar Index exposure where its unit of

trading being one contract equals to $1,000 times index value, ticket size being 0.005

index points at standard contract size, and with quotation in US Dollar Index points,

as computed to the three decimal places (ICE 2025).This futures contract aligns with

the investment horizon with contract series opening in quarterly expiration cycle and

in four months in March, June, September, and December. The given time could fit

the last quarter from July to December.

The amount of hedge (exposure) is determined based on the foreign exchange beta

of -0.974 (𝛽'( ). As illustrated in the previous sections, a negative FX beta implies that

the stock performance is negatively associated with the USD appreciation. With 1

million investments in stock, the USD exposure being: 1,000,000 * 0.974 = USD

$ 974,000.

The number of contracts being USD $974,000/(1,000*100) = 9.74 = 10 contracts

Given the negative foreign exchange beta where the USD appreciation being

disadvantageous to the AES’s stock performance, to hedge the one million

investments in the stock, it is recommended to sell the US Dollar Index Futures

contracts to offset the future stock losses due to the appreciation of USD. Should

there is USD depreciation, then this indicates that the value of US Dollar Index

Futures contracts may fall. In this case, it is recommended to short on the US Dollar

Index Futures contracts to settle for the future gain during the investment horizon.

This adheres with the regression model as indicated in the previous section, where

the foreign currency exposure could be effectively dealt with.
